_readme: Pixar Ads Studio — single source of truth for all six tools. Unofficial fan-concept demo: not affiliated with, endorsed by, or connected to Pixar or Disney. The film slate is fictional. Edit this file and every tool follows.
tagline: Every ad should feel like the first ten minutes of a great film.
one_liner: We market films the way we make them: story first, heart always, never a cheap trick.
brand_brief: Audiences don't buy tickets to animation, they buy tickets to feeling something. Our marketing earns attention the same way our films do: a real character, a real want, an obstacle you recognise from your own life. If an ad wouldn't make an animator proud, it doesn't ship.
disclaimer: Unofficial concept demo. Not affiliated with Pixar or Disney. All films shown are fictional.

positioning.who: Families, animation fans, and grown adults who cry in the first ten minutes and pretend they didn't.
positioning.promise: Ads with the same emotional honesty as the films.
differentiators: Story-first creative: every asset has a character and a want, not a feature list
No spoiler policy: we sell the feeling, never the third act
House palette and type locked in every asset, in every territory

values: Earn the emotion, never force it

audience.core: Parents planning the family weekend, 28-45, deciding between three films and a trampoline park.
also: Animation devotees who see everything opening weekend and post about sakuga
Nostalgic adults, 25-40, who grew up on the classics and go without kids
Teens who discover films through edits and memes, not trailers
Grandparents looking for a safe, genuinely good shared outing

voice.register_default: Warm, wondrous, a little playful. Like a great storyteller at a dinner table, not a hype machine.
we_sound_like: Somebody who loves these characters telling you why
A note from the story room, not a press release
Specific and visual: name the character, the moment, the feeling
we_never_sound_like: A movie-trailer announcer ('One studio. One summer. One unforgettable ride.')
Corporate synergy speak or franchise math
Desperate: begging for tickets, stacking superlatives
signature_moves: Lead with a character's want, not the film's premise
One concrete image beats three adjectives
Let quiet moments be quiet; whitespace is confidence
do: Trust the audience to get it

colours._note: Premiere-night palette. Midnight is the canvas, lamplight is the hero accent. These hex values are mirrored 1:1 in src/index.css tokens; change both together.

typography.headline: Fredoka, rounded and friendly, the warmth of hand-lettering with the discipline of a grid
typography.social_posts: Big rounded display headline, one line of body, wordmark small in a corner
rules: Never condense or outline the headline face

look_and_feel: Premiere night: deep midnight blues, one warm pool of lamplight, characters small against big worlds. Flat colour-block posters with a single strong silhouette beat busy collages every time.

image_rules.signature_layout: One hero silhouette or motif against a flat midnight or accent field, headline set big in the rounded display face, wordmark small in one corner.
image_rules.palette_direction: Midnight blue canvas, lamplight yellow as the hero accent, at most two supporting accents (ember, sky, teal, rose, violet). Flat colour-blocks, no gradients, no lens flares.
ai_must_not_generate: Photorealistic humans presented as cast or fans
Fake critic quotes, award laurels, or star ratings
image_rules.people: Characters appear only as approved silhouettes or supplied art. AI never invents a character's face; audiences spot off-model art instantly and it reads as bootleg.
image_rules.why: Off-model art is the animation equivalent of AI teeth: one wrong frame and trust is gone.

marketing_standards._note: House rules for film marketing claims. Flags are for human review, not legal advice.
hard_rules: Never spoil anything beyond the first act; no third-act imagery, twists, or character fates
Critic quotes must be real, attributed, and unedited; never paraphrase praise into quote marks
Never invent or imply review scores, awards, or 'certified' badges
Rating (G/PG etc.) must be shown accurately; never market a film against its rating
Ticket and offer pricing must be complete: no hidden fees, clear territory and date limits

claim_check._note: Words and claims Story Guard treats as banned or requiring proof.
must_substantiate: award mentions (name the award and year)
critic quotes (name the critic and outlet)
price_and_finance: Family-bundle pricing must name what's included

anti_slop._source: Same empirical no-slop ruleset as the original studio, tuned for film marketing.
hard_bans: three-part power lists ('laughter, tears, and wonder')
trailer-voice openers ('In a world', 'This summer', 'Get ready')
the words: delve, unlock, elevate, seamless, journey, game-changer, unforgettable, epic
fake question pivots ('Looking for the perfect family night? Look no further')
positive_markers: A named character with a concrete want
One specific visual moment from act one
Plain named feelings ('you'll miss your dad', not 'an emotional rollercoaster')
Sentences a human would say out loud
anti_slop.prove_it_test: Could only this film have made this claim? If any film could run this line, cut it.
lint: Max one exclamation mark per post, ideally zero
Read it aloud; if it sounds like a trailer, rewrite

story_guard_scoring._note: Categories and tiers for Story Guard. Weakest category drags the overall (CSD scoring).

categories.name: Story & heart
categories.what: Is there a character, a want, a feeling? Or just marketing about marketing?

categories.name: Claim validity
categories.what: Spoiler-safe, no invented quotes/scores/awards, rating accurate, offers complete.

categories.name: Brand voice
categories.what: Warm storyteller register, lexicon respected, no trailer-voice.

categories.name: No-slop
categories.what: Zero banned patterns; concrete beats generic; human cadence.

story_guard_scoring.feedback: Quote the exact snippet, say what is wrong, give the fix.
story_guard_scoring.output: overall, per-category scores, categorized feedback, on-brand rewrite

films.name: Luma
films.logline: A young firefly who is afraid of the dark must guide her swarm home after the harvest lights go out.
films.audience_focus: Families with young kids; adults who were once afraid of the dark
motifs: a single warm light in deep blue night

films.name: The Deep End
films.logline: A shy octopus who can perfectly imitate anything joins a small-town swim team and has to finally be himself.
films.audience_focus: Kids 8-12, teens, anyone who ever hid in plain sight

films.name: Wrench & Rivet
films.logline: Two rival repair robots inherit the same junkyard and must rebuild it together before the city reclaims it.

films.name: Paper Moon
films.logline: A grandmother and granddaughter fold one thousand paper cranes to keep a promise neither fully understands.

true_claims: Fictional slate: Luma (summer), The Deep End (autumn), Wrench & Rivet (spring), Paper Moon (winter)
Family ticket bundles exist in participating cinemas; terms shown in-asset
Behind-the-scenes 'story room' content is real and produced in-house

demo_captions._note: Used by the Story Guard golden examples.
demo_captions.bad: This summer, get ready for the most EPIC, mind-blowing animated event of the year!!! Critics agree: Luma is a guaranteed instant classic — laughter, tears, and wonder for the whole family! Don't miss the unforgettable journey everyone is talking about! #Luma #MustSee #InstantClassic
demo_captions.why_bad: Trailer-voice opener, invented critic consensus, banned superlatives, em dash, three-part list, hashtag stuffing.
demo_captions.good: Luma is scared of the dark. Tonight she's the only light her swarm has. Bring the kid who still checks under the bed; this one's for them. In cinemas this summer.
demo_captions.why_good: Named character with a concrete want, one act-one image, plain feeling, speaks to one parent, no slop patterns.
